sentences.text: There was a group like the partisans.
The partisans, they took a lot of young people, like if my cousin was a partisan, he come, he could take his sister.
He could take his mother, if they wanted to go.
And he come, from the partisan, it's true.
But they didn't want to go.
This was the group from the partisans, what I remember.
Another groups, I don't know.
The partisan group, no.
No, I remember there was-- they used to keep in the ghetto yellow signs, and this sign, who gets the yellow sign has a right to leave.
So my father get a yellow sign, because he was working in the German place.
And I was-- when we leave the ghetto, we have to leave-- there was like an exit, you know?
So when we leave the ghetto, you have to have the yellow sign with you.
Then you have the right to walk out of the ghetto.
The people what's left in to ghetto, they was all shoot in Ponary.
So my father had a yellow sign.
He took me, and my brother, and my mother.
Just, I was dressed so much clothes on me, living in the ghetto, that I looked like his wife, not like his sister-- like his-- like his daughter.
So they took me straight away-- away to kill me.
And my father and my brother and my-- and my brother, went out-- out the ghetto.
And my father didn't lose control.
He come up straight away to the German, and he said, look, I'm working for this-and-this German.
Why they took my wife away?
So he come back, and they take me out from the-- from the group where they shoot-- have to shoot.
And where was your mother?
My mother was already out.
Because I was looking like two mothers there, my mother went through with my brother.
And me, he said, what two wives you have?
So he threw-- threw me out-- to shoot me.
So then he didn't lose control.
He come back, and take me out.
Pardon?
There was a Judenrat, yes.
The Judenrat, he was called, his name was [?
Gens. ?] He said, he will stay in the ghetto.
Even he take up, two Jewish people, to go from the ghetto.
But he wanted-- he wanted to take-- he was very good.
But he didn't have a choice.
Because they killed him exactly like they kill somebody else, to the liquidation from the ghetto, there was no, nobody left.
Not me, my husband, yes.
Because he-- he had a Polish woman outside the ghetto, because he know her.
He was working with her before the war in the same factory.
So he always go through the-- through the-- through the old homes, you know, there used to be high ceilings.
You can walk from a house to another, till you got out of the ghetto.
So when he went out from the ghetto, he went over to this woman, to the Polish woman.
And she used to go to the field and put potatoes for them.
When the potatoes were ready, she used to go with him, and take the potatoes.
So he used to climb back, bring them to the ghetto.
But officially he couldn't bring them through the door to the ghettos.
So he has one, what he had help a lot.
And was there any corruption in the ghetto?
What do you mean corruption?
No, yes there was.
Like they come once, they come, they said, they need a thousand people, the Germans, they need a thousand people.
So the-- the commissar, the [INAUDIBLE] commissar, from the ghetto, what they provide in the ghetto, he has to bring-- he has to give the Germans a thousand people.
So they used to hiding in the hiding places.
They used to run away.
They looked for himself, everybody most of them mens, no womans.
Who decided?
The German come to one was provided with the ghetto, the [?
Gens. ?] They said to him, I need 1,000 people for tomorrow.
And you have to give it to me.
They trust him.
But do they have another choice?
There was.
There was a lot.
There was Jewish police, which some of them, they was horrible.
Because if somebody went out to work, outside the ghetto, you always bring something hiding.
They used to make in the coats behind, big pockets, to put some food in to bring for the family in the ghetto.
So there were some of them, they used to come and touch you.
Ah, what do you have here?
Leave it.
Took it out, and let him go to the-- into the ghetto.
But there was.
There was-- there was from the-- from the Vilna people, from it-- that was in the-- in the police.
And how did the people react?
There was-- they had no-- they react.
They said to him, how can you do it?
You're standing by the door from the ghetto.
You said, don't you know me?
Each other know very well.
Don't you know me?
He said, I don't know you.
I have to I have my-- done my job.
Because he was afraid for himself, in case the Germans will check.
Then he will-- he will be one which will be responsible too.
No.
I married in 1943, the 17th of April, in ghetto.
People were wondering, what do you mean?
You're getting married in ghetto?
This is something what's unusual to hear things like that.
Just, we was very much in love.
And like, all that time, you-- you didn't trust each other so good.
So we had-- we come to my parents.
And I said to mom, look.
I'm getting married.
So she said to me, what are you talking about?
Who's getting married now?
What-- you haven't got time.
It's-- we passed the Germans.
If we were in a normal world, I'll make you a normal chuppah.
You don't know what life means.
What do you mean, you're going to married?
I said, Mom, we love each other.
We're going to get married.
So, she said all right.
You get married.
I'll arrange with [? mazel. ?] So we had a chuppah.
Because my husband has a little place to live by himself, a very small place over by himself.
So we took that.
There was no rabbi.
There was like a shamus.
We took him there, and we said to him, look, we're going to get married.
So my father was working outside the ghetto.
So I had two rings, what he make them.
But inside in the rings is written 'ghetto' and the date what I marry.
And he done this outside the ghetto, and bring us in the ghetto.
And we have a chuppah.
And we have [NON-ENGLISH], just nothing from this is left from [NON-ENGLISH].
And the met up in the ghetto.
And we stay maybe six weeks together, and I was pregnant, straight away after I marry, not before marriage.
There was no such a thing, after marriage.
And they was not allowed to have children in ghetto.
You're not allowed.
Because in that way you get shot straight away.
So my mother was crying.
She said, your life, you would have no children.
No children you will have.
If you're going to make now, it's finished.
I said, we have to do it.
I can-- I can't go like that in the ghetto.
So I went into-- there was a hospital in the ghetto.
And I went into the doctor, Dr.
Seles, and I said to him, look my mother is crying.
She said, if it's [NON-ENGLISH], and I will live on, and I want to have children, I will never have children.
And he said, tell your mother that she will always remember.
I will do you like that, that you will have girls and boys.
Don't you worry about it.
Just we did an ascetic.
And my father bring it.
Through this German, he give it to him.
He bought it and bring in an ascetic, and they give me through an ascetic.
Yes, he was in ghetto at this time, yeah.
And then there was, after six weeks, they took him.
He was hiding in the place where we was living.
Just he said, no.
I have enough.
I have to go there, and have a look what is outside.
And soon-- soon he went out, and when there, they catch him, and send him to the concentration camp.
There was a medical facility in the ghetto, yes, there was.
Not for very serious, you know.
First, you have to have the money there.
The doctors were very good.

But the doctor there, you don't need it really for a doctor, like you need for the-- you need antibiotics, like something like that, what didn't-- there was no antibiotic-- but something what protect you, needed money to buy.
There was a hospital, but not for very, very sick people.
Very, very sick people, they would get killed.
Because the German used to come and check.
Not really.
Not really.
Most of them, I think, the older people, you know, the older people.
The ghetto, really didn't arrive-- to our ghetto, didn't come nowhere from other places, only Vilna, only from the town.
There used to be ghettos where they used to bring from-- from another part, from Poland.
No, they didn't bring to our ghetto.
To our ghetto was only Vilna.
So every time there was less and less and less.
Yes.
Deportation start approximately, like in-- this was in 1943, in September-- it was the 23rd of September.
Why I remember so good the day?
Because every time it's coming the [NON-ENGLISH] from the liquidation from ghetto is the 23rd of September 1943.
Then the liquid-- there was not many people already left in Vilna ghetto.
But still there was enough.
And then they bring us to a field in-- in Vilna.
That was already prepared for us with-- with gates closed up.
And we was there maybe a couple of nights, something like that.
It was cold and rainy.
And then they start to select us to go out from the-- from there.
And they select us right and left.
This was the liquidation from the ghetto.
And I was going together with my mother and mother-in-law, keep them together.
And when we come to the gate, the German was standing.
They took away my mother and my mother-in-law, and threw them on the left, and for me, they threw me on the right.
So I had a watch on my hand.
And I said to the German, from-- from far away to my mother.
I said mom, I'm going to give German the watch, and I'm coming to you.
I'm not going without you.
And she started-- at first the German didn't took me, pushed me back.
And she started to scream from far away.
Don't you dare come to us.
We're all going to shoot.
Because we are old people.
And you young, and it will be a normal world.
You meet your husband, and you will be alive.
Don't you dare come to me.
The German didn't let me.
I would go.
I wouldn't listen to her.
Who-- who leaves their mother?
So I was lucky.
They didn't-- they just-- they take me there.
I went to the-- to the right.
And then we-- we were concentrated to the camps.
So your father and your brother--
My father-- my father and my brother went for the mens.
They were separate.
We-- they didn't permit a mens together.
By the liquidation from ghetto, they make already, two separate places, one for the womans, one for the mens.
So I-- when I went out from the ghetto, this was it.
I never saw my brother and my father.
My brother went with my father.
Now my father was very young looking, and they put him on the right, with my brother together, to live-- to live.
Just it was bad luck that he was very sick.
And he went in, there was actually in the concentration camp for children, and he was 13 years old.
So they took him.
And my father was very sick, and then they come to take the whole hospital, and shot him, all of them.
Because my husband come two days later.
If he would come two days earlier, they would run away with him together.
And when he come, he ask, have you hear something about Kaplan?
So they told him.
This was in Estonia.
I don't know exactly the name from the-- from the concentration camp.
I went from there.
They took us to Riga.
Over there, there was a big concentration camp.
But there was-- there-- from there, they used to take the-- the what was more healthy one, and send it to work.
Like I went from Riga to Strasdenhof.
Over there we went in 2,000 young woman in this camp.
And we was staying there about nearly-- nearly a year.
And there they put us to work.
I had a very good job from the start.
I was working in-- in the houses-- like they're putting under the glass, these [NON-ENGLISH] they call.
They used to have tomatoes, and cucumber, grapes-- everything in the warm houses.
And then I was working two-- two [INAUDIBLE] woman, they used to bring me every morning a sandwich.
And I said to them, look, can I have a half, and half I bring for my cousin to the camp?
She said, no.
You don't take nothing from here.
Because if they catch you, they catch me.
And we both lost.
So I couldn't bring my cousin nothing.
Then we had a-- a kapo.
She was provided with our camp in Strasdenhof.
And she said to me, look.
[NON-ENGLISH], then they called, [NON-ENGLISH], if you can't bring any cucumber and tomatoes and everything was there, I take you off from the job.
You will never have this job.
And I said, look, I have here a cousin, first cousin.
She's laying.
She have nothing to eat.
And I can't take it.
I can't.
I will risk it with my life.
I can't do it.
So she said to me, if you can't do it, I'll find somebody that will.
So she took me off of the job, and she put somebody else there.
And then the other woman, the person done it.
I couldn't.
I was afraid, maybe, this was-- I couldn't.
So I back, and then when she took me off from this job, they put me in a-- in a factory for parachute, working on the machines, making this material.
Over there, we were staying 12 hours a day.
Do you remember the name of the lady, the kapo?
Yes.
She-- her name was Freda Vilinski.
She was here in Melbourne.
I mean, I saw her here.
And we wanted really to take her to court.
But there was not enough witness.
There was more witness for her like against her.
By trains, that's how.
By train.
We went-- there was all young-- all young people, young woman there, only womans.
And there was Ukraine, Ukraine-Lithuanian, Polish soldiers.
And they done-- with the young, which no was married.
You know, I'm married.
So they had nothing what to look for me.
They ask you.
Are you married?
I said, yes, I'm married.
So the girls was innocent girls, they make with them whatever they wanted.
That was a terrible, terrible place on this-- in this-- in the trains, shocking.
It was a train for cattle, so we was like-- we was handled just like cattle.
All on-- only-- all on them.
All in-- all in this train.
They didn't take, in front of everybody.
Stand and not look at them.
Nothing, what can you do?
You just stand and look at them, what are they doing to you, to them.
I was really lucky.
I wasn't such a nice-looking one.
There was beautiful girls, tall, nice, beautiful, young.
They come back with us to the concentration camp.
What can they do?
They come back.
Some of them couldn't hold it.
There was a lot of them was dead in, in, in in the train.
The was one girl, not in-- in the train.
It was in the concentration camp, in Strasdenhof.
There was one, he was a prisoner of war too, a German.
And he fall in love with-- with a girl in the concentration camp.
And she's from Vilna too.
And they fall in love, and they live with-- with them together.
And she has everything, because he had everything, to eat and everything, and she was pregnant.
And then, after when they liquidated the concentration camp, this Strasdenhof, she was killed.
Kaiserwald.
For there, it was a short time.
There was only-- because we went from there to other concentration camps around-- around the Kaiserwald, you know, not far away.
So that's what the Strasdenhof-- that was a terrible like concentration camp.
There was-- they had to shoot to till 18 years all, and from 30 years.
So between 18 and 30, they have the right to live.
What they done it-- they put us in-- in a selection.
They took us out all from the-- from the rooms.
And set an appell on a yard.
And they took off.
[NON-ENGLISH] you go to work, you go to work.
So we went out.
And I had a girlfriend that she was with me in the same age.
And she started scream, Rachel, you know that I'm the same age.
I had a mistake in writing.
What shall I do?
I went up to the-- to the lady that we was provided-- and I said to her, look.
She's with me in the same age.
She written.
I can do nothing.
And when we come back there was nothing, no more, only the people that were right between 18 and 30 to live.
No.
We didn't have different clothes.
They bring the clothes from the dead people, and tell us to change.
Yes.
We did have-- in Strasdenhof to shave our hair, because we could run-- in case we run away, so they know we are there, we didn't have-- we didn't have any hair.
Even after the liberation, I still come without hair back.
In Kaiserwald, it was a very short time.
Because from there, they used to send all the people to work.
It was a-- a mind center for people to go to work.
And then the German come, and they ask the people.
Give me 1,000 people, give me 2,000 people, and they took them to work.
Some of them, they had very good camp.
They was working in the field, you know.
So there was-- they was there living there.
You know, they didn't bring them back, just working on the fields.
Nearly a year.
First thing in the morning they make appell.
They call us all up.
And one, one come, the German, one come and said, look, you have to give us all the children till 18 and from 30.
This was the appell once.
And then another one, they bring some soup.
And they said, look, we bring something to eat.
If you eat, you'll be all right.
And I was a very small eater at home.
I could live without food.
I taste it.
I didn't like it.
Who eat it was very, very sick and didn't recover.
Most of them, they used to be-- they used to give you a piece of bread, a slice of bread.
It could be for a day.
That would be for two days.
And every day, we take a small piece.
They used to-- the woman who got work from the outside the camp, and like somebody had something left, like a-- a ring.
You said, here is my ring.
Bring me some bread.
So they used handle ghetto.
And they come.
They said to me, you know what?
Look, we're going.
Where's a good place to hide the bread, what are we're going there.
She said, put in with Rachel.
In Rachel's bed, nobody will take it.
They know she hasn't got nothing.
And she wouldn't touch it.
And I didn't.
It was lying-- loaves of bread.
I was sleeping on it.
And nothing would be wrong if I took a knife or break a piece and eat it.
I didn't done it.
I couldn't.
I was not the type.
I really-- I was really lucky to survive.
Because people like me was very hard to survive.
I was really lucky, because of them, people in concentration camp has to know you have bread, take it.
Who-- what can she do to you?
Nothing.
What can she do?
Nothing.
Next time she wouldn't do it.
But I couldn't do this.
And this, I think-- however, I don't believe now, because this happened with my daughter.
But then I believe it.
It is our God that's helped me through go such things?
Because my mother used to say, goodness me, how can you live?
You're so skinny, your bones will fall to pieces, so skinny I was.
And the girls that survived, were they bigger?
Where they stronger?
Were they smaller?
No.
No.
The girls who-- who survived was smaller girls like me, most of them.
Because the healthy one couldn't take the hunger.
They was very strong, and they eat it.
They needed it.
We passed by a lot of them, the young-- like we went-- when I-- when we come to it.
It was 2,000 young woman that went.
But the liberation might be 200.
There was a toilet.
There was a toilet.
And there was-- what shall I tell you?
This is like-- like a-- like you put the spare thing on it-- on it.
This was long one, and there was a tap with cold water.
You could wash yourself with the cold water.
So in the nighttime, you cover with your blanket, took everything out, you wash under the cold water, keep them under the mattress.
And in the morning, we wear it.
There was 1, 2, 3 4-- four rows like that, you know, one under another.
And what can I describe?
It was just-- I was sleeping together with my cousin on the same-- on the same piece of metal that was there, made from nothing.
And there was girls where they was very energetic in-- in-- in-- in the concentration camp.
They used to have contact with the Germans.
They couldn't care less, if they have a good-- if they have a good time and had something to eat, you know.
Not everybody was so stupid, like me.
They couldn't care less.
They lived for the day.
And I was living with the day, that tomorrow if I will be alive, I meet my husband.
That's what I was living.
There was 2,000 girls.
There was only one, a very big one.
There was three, 1, 2, 3.
What do mean?
We didn't have, who had?
As soon as I left ghetto.
What I was-- I was such a-- such a big woman?
We straight away we lost it.
We lost it.
I didn't have it, till I come home.
Germans.
Germans, and I tell you worse, Lithuanian, Ukraine.
They was the worst, worse like the Germans.
Because they was antisemites, real antisemites from before they hate the Jews.
And now when they have the opportunity to kill them--
You has to be lucky to the guard too.
If a guard liked you, he used to bring even a sandwich in the morning to eat.
If he doesn't like you, you would work in there like-- like 10 horses.
Nothing.
We were so tired after 12 hours working, and sometimes nothing to eat, that we're lying on a-- on a-- on the bed, and sleep, like till tomorrow, 5 o'clock in the morning.
We was only thinking one thing.
When the Russians come, the first bomb to throw on us.
That was our-- that was our-- what we wanted to do, not to suffer anymore.
That's what we wanted.
Not, really.
We know each other, everybody.
But everybody have so much problems there.
Who think-- who was thinking.
I was keeping only with my cousin together.
Like everybody has a friend.
Some of them had friends that keep with friends together.
Some of them had family, keeping the family together.
My cousin's name was Lila Kuszner.
I don't know.
She's the one in Israel, yes.
Once I remember, they used to come.
Somebody come from Estonia, to our lager, to our concentration camp.
And he said, what-- he come up to me and ask me, who are you?
I said, I'm Drabkin.
You Drabkin?
I saw your-- I saw your husband, the father, the father with the five brothers.
This was the first time what I hear something about him.
